message:
fix(fcp-sandbox): reject non-http(s) schemes and embedded credentials in HTTP egress URLs

`EgressGuard::evaluate_http` previously parsed the request URL with
`url::Url::parse` and then fell through to the host/port/TLS check
without validating either the scheme or the authority's userinfo. Two
real-world gaps fall out of that:

1. **Non-http(s) schemes were partially accepted.** The code derived
   `tls_required = url.scheme() == "https"` but otherwise treated any
   parseable URL the same way. A URL like `ftp://files.example.com/data`
   would be denied only incidentally, via the port allow-list (ftp's
   default port 21 happens not to be in the baseline allow-list). If a
   caller's port_allow list happened to include 21 — for example for an
   audited FTP tunnel — the guard would then let an `ftp://` URL pass
   through a code path that unconditionally treats the request as HTTP.
   We now fail closed up front: anything that is not `http` or `https`
   is rejected with `EgressError::InvalidUrl`.

2. **Embedded userinfo bypassed host checks.** A URL of the form
   `https://user:pass@api.example.com/data` is legal per RFC 3986 and
   `url::Url` happily parses it, exposing `host_str() = "api.example.com"`.
   That passes the host allow-list even though the effective auth
   identity is attacker-controlled. Worse, any credential the caller had
   already pinned via `credential_id` is silently shadowed by the URL's
   userinfo when the request reaches a reqwest client. Rather than
   second-guess every downstream client, we reject any HTTP egress URL
   whose `username()` is non-empty or whose `password()` is `Some` at
   guard time.

Both rules fail closed with `EgressError::InvalidUrl`, which the caller
already surfaces as a deny-with-reason rather than a panic or an
unhandled error path.

Test changes:

- `test_evaluate_http_custom_scheme_no_default_port` renamed to
  `test_evaluate_http_rejects_non_http_scheme_even_when_port_allowed`
  and updated to put 21 into `port_allow` so the assertion is now
  specifically "scheme gate fires even when ports would let it through"
  instead of the previous "port gate happens to deny it".
- `test_evaluate_http_rejects_embedded_credentials` added to cover the
  userinfo rejection on an otherwise allowed host.
- Pure `cargo fmt` reflows elsewhere in the file (multi-line
  `assert!(matches!(...))` bodies and one long `assert!` call) came
  along for the ride — no behavioural change in those hunks.
